title: The Scientist Who Couldn't Explain It: Kundalini and the Limits of Materialism
meta_description: You're probably here because you're terrified. You're feeling things, seeing things, hearing things, that make absolutely no sense. Your body's doing stuff
body:
The Scientist Who Couldn't Explain It: Kundalini and the Limits of Materialism

You're probably here because you're terrified. You're feeling things, seeing things, hearing things, that make absolutely no sense. Your body's doing stuff you can't control. Your mind is a broken record player, skipping between panic and a weird, buzzing clarity. You're alone, it's 3 AM, and you're Googling "am I going crazy" or "what is happening to me." I've been there. I know that feeling in your gut, that cold dread that tells you everything you thought you knew about reality is just... gone. You're not crazy. Or maybe you are, but you're not alone in it. And here's the thing: what you're experiencing, it might just be Kundalini. And yeah, it can feel like your brain is melting and your soul is being ripped apart.

I was a scientist. Not a spiritual guru, not a mystic, just a guy who believed in data, in testable hypotheses, in things I could measure and quantify. My world was built on logic, on cause and effect, on the idea that if you couldn't put it under a microscope or prove it with an equation, it probably wasn't real. My whole identity was wrapped up in that approach. I thought I knew how the universe worked, how the human mind worked. I was so sure. And then, well, then Kundalini hit me like a freight train made of pure energy and existential dread. And it blew every single one of my carefully constructed beliefs to smithereens. It wasn't gentle. It wasn't pretty. It was a brutal, terrifying, and ultimately, deeply altering experience that forced me to confront the absolute limits of materialism. The limits of what my scientific mind could even begin to process.

You're probably looking for answers, for some kind of rational explanation for the chaos inside you. And I'm going to tell you, straight up, that a purely materialist worldview just won't cut it here. It can't. It doesn't have the language, the concepts, or even the willingness to admit that something like this can happen. That's why you feel so alone. The doctors you might see, the therapists - bless their hearts, they're doing their best - but if they haven't been through it, or at least studied it extensively, they're going to try and fit your experience into a box they understand: psychosis, anxiety, mood disorder. And you know, deep down, that's not quite right. Right? It's something else. It's bigger. It's... cosmic, in the most unsettling way possible.

The Disintegration of My Reality

I remember the first time it really kicked in. It wasn't a slow build. It was like a switch flipped. One moment, I was just me, living my life, worrying about deadlines and bills. The next, my body was buzzing, vibrating with an internal electricity I couldn't explain. I felt heat moving through my spine, sometimes like a searing fire, other times like cool water. My muscles would twitch and jerk involuntarily. I'd have spontaneous movements, my body contorting into strange yoga-like postures I'd never practiced. My sleep became a battleground of vivid, often terrifying dreams, and then, eventually, no sleep at all. My senses went haywire. Sounds were too loud, colors too bright. I'd see auras, flashes of light, geometric patterns behind my eyelids. I heard internal sounds - ringing, humming, sometimes even voices, though they weren't like external voices. It was all internal, all consuming.

As a scientist, my first instinct was to analyze. To observe. To try and find a logical explanation. Was it a neurological disorder? A brain tumor? A rare autoimmune disease? I ran tests. Lots of tests. MRIs, EEGs, blood work, everything. And everything came back... normal. Perfectly normal. My brain, according to the machines, was fine. My body was fine. But I was definitely not fine. I was a walking, talking, vibrating contradiction to everything I believed about objective reality. My scientific training told me, "If it's not measurable, it's not real." But how could I deny what I was experiencing so viscerally? How could I deny the intense emotional swings, the sudden bouts of bliss followed by crushing despair, the feeling of my identity dissolving, only to be replaced by something vast and impersonal? It was terrifying. I genuinely thought I was losing my mind, that I was having a psychotic break. And the worst part was, I couldn't tell anyone who would understand. Who would believe me? They'd just lock me up.

I remember trying to explain it to a colleague, using scientific terms, trying to find analogies. "It's like a massive energy discharge in my central nervous system," I'd say, "but there's no physical correlate." He just looked at me with polite concern, the kind you give to someone who's clearly unwell. That's when I realized I was on my own. My scientific community, my intellectual home, had no room for this. It was outside their purview. It was anomalous. It was, quite simply, beyond their scope. And that's where you are right now, isn't it? Feeling like you're an anomaly, like you're broken in a way no one can fix, because the tools they have aren't designed for this particular kind of breakage. Or, more accurately, this particular kind of transformation.

Finding a Language Beyond Science

I started devouring everything I could find that even hinted at such experiences. I went down rabbit holes I never would have touched before. Ancient texts, esoteric traditions, spiritual philosophies. And that's when I stumbled upon the word: Kundalini. And suddenly, a tiny, flickering light appeared in the overwhelming darkness. There were others. Not just a few, but countless people throughout history, across cultures, who had described almost exactly what I was going through. The intense heat, the involuntary movements, the visions, the emotional purging, the feeling of energy rising up the spine. It had a name. It had a context. It had a history. That was a huge relief, honestly. Just knowing I wasn't the only one, that I wasn't just inventing some elaborate delusion.

I found books that spoke directly to my experience. Books like The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link). Sannella, a psychiatrist, actually documented case studies of people experiencing these phenomena, people who were often misdiagnosed with psychiatric conditions. His work was a lifeline. It showed me that there was a bridge, however fragile, between the scientific and the spiritual. Then there was The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link), which offered practical advice and a deeper understanding of the process. These weren't New Age fluff; they were serious attempts to make sense of something incredibly powerful and disruptive. And they validated my experience in a way no medical doctor could.

I also came across the concept of "spiritual emergency," a term coined by Stanislav and Christina Grof. Their book, The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link), was another crucial piece of the puzzle. It explained how intense spiritual experiences can sometimes be indistinguishable from psychiatric crises, and how crucial it is to differentiate between the two. Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link), another Grof work, further cemented this understanding. They weren't saying it was all in my head, or that I was just having a bad trip. They were saying, "This is real. It's difficult. And it requires a different kind of support." Know what I mean? It's not about being 'sick'; it's about being in a process that's overwhelming everything you thought you were.

The Limits of Materialism

Here's the brutal truth: materialism, the belief that only matter and energy exist, and that all phenomena can ultimately be explained by physical processes, has a hard ceiling. It's great for building bridges, curing diseases, and understanding the mechanics of the universe. But it utterly fails when confronted with consciousness itself, with subjective experience, and with phenomena like Kundalini. It simply doesn't have the tools. It tries to reduce everything to brain chemistry, to neural networks, to electrical impulses. And while those things are certainly involved - your brain IS doing something different during this - they don't explain the *experience* of it. They don't explain the deep insights, the sense of interconnectedness, the feeling of a presence, or the sheer, undeniable energetic reality of it all.

My own experience forced me to confront this head-on. I couldn't deny what was happening to me. It was too real, too persistent, too powerful. And yet, my scientific training offered no satisfactory explanation. It was like trying to explain the taste of an apple by only describing its atomic structure. You're missing the entire experience. You're missing the consciousness that perceives the taste. You're missing the 'what it's like' of it all. And Kundalini is ALL 'what it's like'. It's pure, raw, unfiltered experience. It demands that you expand your definition of reality, whether you want to or not. It forces you to consider that there are dimensions of existence, of consciousness, that lie beyond the reach of our current scientific instruments and theories. That there's more to you than just your body and your brain. That there's an animating force, an energy, that science hasn't quite figured out how to measure, but that you're feeling right now, intensely.

Does that land? Does it make sense that the very approach you might be clinging to - the need for a rational, scientific explanation - is exactly what's making this experience so much more terrifying? Because it's telling you that what you're feeling can't be real. But it IS real. And you know it. Your body knows it. Your screaming nerves know it.

What To Do When Science Fails You

So, what do you do when your trusted scientific worldview crumbles? You find a new way to understand. You don't abandon reason, not entirely, but you expand it. You embrace the mystery. You seek out others who have walked this path, who speak this strange language of energy and spirit. You learn to listen to your body, to the energy moving within you, instead of trying to fight it or explain it away with inadequate terms.

  • Acceptance is key. This isn't going away by ignoring it. Fighting it often makes it worse. Try, just try, to lean into the discomfort, the fear. Tell yourself, "This is happening. I don't understand it, but it's happening."
  • Grounding practices. This is huge. You're likely feeling unmoored, disconnected. Get outside. Touch the earth. Eat nourishing food. Take warm baths. Focus on your breath. Anything that brings you back into your physical body and out of the swirling chaos in your head.
  • Seek out experienced guides. Not just anyone. Someone who has been through Kundalini awakening themselves, or has extensive experience guiding others through it. Be discerning. There are charlatans out there. Look for stability, compassion, and common sense. Someone who doesn't promise quick fixes, but offers practical, grounded support.
  • Read, read, read. The books I mentioned are a great start. There are many others. Educate yourself about what's happening. The more you understand the process, the less terrifying it becomes. Knowledge, even non-scientific knowledge, can be incredibly empowering.
  • Be patient. This isn't a sprint. It's a marathon. A long, arduous, and sometimes incredibly painful journey. There are no shortcuts. It takes as long as it takes. And you'll have good days and bad days. Many, many bad days.
  • Don't self-diagnose psychiatric illness. While it feels like psychosis, it's usually not. If you are truly concerned, see a doctor, but be aware they may not understand what you're going through. Advocate for yourself. Explain that you believe it's a spiritual emergency.

I know this sounds like a lot. And when you're in the thick of it, just getting through the next hour feels impossible. But here's what I want you to hold onto: you're not broken. You're not alone. And what you're experiencing, as terrifying as it is, is a process of deep, deep transformation. It's stripping away everything that isn't you, everything that's holding you back, everything that's false. And that process is violent. It's messy. It's unbearable at times. But on the other side of it, there's a new you waiting. A more authentic, more aware, more connected you. One that understands the universe in a way your old scientific mind could never have conceived. I'm telling you, I've been there. I'm still integrating. And it gets better. It really does. You just have to hang on.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
